methods.method_name: Sediment Sampling and Heavy Metal Analysis
methods.description: This method describes the process of collecting marine sediment samples, preparing them through drying, grinding, and sieving, forming them into pellets, and finally determining the concentration of heavy metals (Mn, Fe, Ni, Cu, Zn, As, Pb) using EDXRF spectrometry. It also includes steps for recording sampling location and environmental parameters, sample storage, and quality assurance/control.

steps.description: Marine sediment samples were gathered using a Van Veen grab sampler onboard the R / V speed research ship in August 2013 from 16 different sampling stations of the Middle and Eastern Black Sea coastal region of Turkey.
steps.searchable_summary: gather marine sediment samples using a grab sampler
inputs: Middle and Eastern Black Sea coastal region

steps.description: At the same time, the position of each sampling point was recorded by the automatic positioning system (GPS) on the research ship.
steps.searchable_summary: record sampling point coordinates using GPS

steps.description: At the points where the samples were taken, temperature and depth parameters were recorded automatically with the help of the CTD multi-parameter probe.
steps.searchable_summary: record temperature and depth using a CTD probe

steps.description: The collected sediment samples were stored in clean polyethylene bags and immediately transferred to the laboratory in the cold chain box for further processing (Şirin 2019).
steps.searchable_summary: store and transport sediment samples in cold chain

steps.description: Firstly, impurities such as plant fragments and stones were removed from sediment samples.
steps.searchable_summary: remove impurities from sediment samples

steps.description: After the sediment samples were weighed on a precision scale to be about 100 g...
steps.searchable_summary: weigh sediment samples

steps.description: ...they were left to dry at 105 o C for 48 h until they reached a constant mass (Karbasdehi et al. 2016;Tholkappian et al. 2018)
steps.searchable_summary: dry sediment samples at 105C until constant mass

steps.description: ...and the samples were ground to a fine powder in a mortar for 15-20 min.
steps.searchable_summary: grind dried sediment samples to fine powder

steps.description: The samples were sieved with a 63 μm sieve, as metals accumulated more as a result of the increase in surface area as the particles became smaller in size (Ravisankar et al. 2014;Kumar et al. 2017;Gholizadeh and Patimar 2018;Ni et al. 2018;Ustaoğlu and Islam 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: sieve ground sediment powder with a 63 μm sieve

steps.description: Prior to metal analysis, the dried samples were placed in a vacuum-evacuated desiccator.
steps.searchable_summary: place dried samples in a vacuum desiccator

steps.description: For measurements, after 4 g of pretreated sediment powder and about 0.5 g of Licowax powder were thoroughly mixed in a mortar to ensure homogeneity...
steps.searchable_summary: mix sediment powder and Licowax for homogeneity
outputs: Homogeneous mixture of sediment and Licowax

steps.description: ...it was pressed into a 40-mm diameter disc (pellet) by a 20 ton hydraulic press machine (Specac, Atlas ™ Manual 25 T).
steps.searchable_summary: press mixture into a pellet using a hydraulic press
inputs: 20 ton hydraulic press (Specac, Atlas ™ Manual 25 T)

steps.description: The contents of heavy metals such as Mn, Fe, Ni, Cu, Zn, As, and Pb in the sediment were determined using EDXRF spectrometer (Epsilon5, PANalytical, Almelo, the Netherlands).
steps.searchable_summary: determine heavy metal content using EDXRF spectrometer
outputs: Heavy metal concentrations (Mn, Fe, Ni, Cu, Zn, As, Pb)

steps.description: Each pellet sample was analyzed in triplicate in the system (Baltas et al. 2017b).
steps.searchable_summary: analyze each pellet sample in triplicate
outputs: Triplicate measurements of heavy metal concentrations

steps.description: The measurement time was taken as 30 min for each pellet sample.
steps.searchable_summary: set EDXRF measurement time per sample

steps.description: As shown in Table S2, quality assurance and control of the method were achieved by using the national certified reference material, the Lake Ontario sediment (NW-WQB-1).
steps.searchable_summary: perform quality assurance and control using certified reference material
inputs: Certified reference material (Lake Ontario sediment NW-WQB-1)

steps.description: The recovery of all metals analyzed in the reference material was between 95.4 and 104.7%.
steps.searchable_summary: check metal recovery against certified reference material values

methods.method_name: Multivariate Analysis of Sediment Heavy Metals
methods.description: This method describes the statistical procedures used to analyze the relationships among heavy metals in sediment and infer their potential sources. It includes calculating Pearson's correlation coefficients and performing Principal Component Analysis (PCA).

steps.description: A matrix of Pearson's correlation coefficients was used to assess the degree of correlation among the metals and to distinguish the sources of the metals in the sediments (Table S5).
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Pearson's correlation matrix for heavy metals and distance

steps.description: The correlation analysis showed that there was a significantly positive relationship between the elemental pairs Cu-Zn (r = 0.973), Zn-Cr (r = 0.960) and Ni-Cr (r = 0.845) at p < 0.01. There was also a moderate positive relationship between Fe-Cu (r = 0.552), Ni-Distance (r = 0.615), and Cr-Distance (r = 0.529) at p < 0.05... But, a moderate negative correlation exists between Fe and Pb (r = −0.574, p < 0.05).
steps.searchable_summary: assess statistical significance of correlations

steps.description: According to the study published by Thollkappian et al. (2018), if the correlation coefficient between metals is positive, these metals are likely to have a common source, interdependence, and the same behavior in the transportation process... Significant negative correlations between some heavy metals indicated that these heavy metals could originate from different pollution sources (Chabukdhara and Nema 2013;Ahamad et al. 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: interpret positive and negative correlations for source inference
outputs: Inferred common or different sources based on correlations

steps.description: PCA analysis, one of the multivariate statistical methods, was also used to determine the relationships between metals in sediment and the sources of metals.
steps.searchable_summary: perform Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

steps.description: Three main components with eigenvalues greater than 1 were identified...
steps.searchable_summary: identify main components based on eigenvalues > 1

steps.description: ...explaining 85.712% of the system variance.
steps.searchable_summary: determine variance explained by each component
outputs: Percentage of variance explained by each component

steps.description: The graphical representation of the three components (PC 1, PC 2 and PC 3) where the relationships between heavy metals can be seen is given in Fig. 3.
steps.searchable_summary: generate graphical representation of PCA components

steps.description: Liu et al. (2003) classified the factor loadings as strong (< 0.75), moderate (0.75-0.50), and weak (0.50-0.30) (Liu et al. 2003;Ustaoğlu and Islam 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: classify PCA factor loadings based on thresholds

steps.description: As reported in Table 4, PC1 (38.503% variance) showed strong positive loading for Cu and Zn and moderate positive loading for Fe... PC2 (31.515% variance) showed a high factor loading on Ni and Cr... PC3 (15.694% variance) showed strong positive and negative loading for Mn and Pb, respectively, while it showed moderately positive loading for Fe... Therefore, PC1 could be better explained as anthropogenic sources such as agricultural and mining... Therefore, the high loading factors detected for Ni and Cr in the PC2 component indicate that the levels of these metals can be attributed primarily to lithogenic effects (natural sources)... Therefore, PC3 could be better explained as the natural and anthropogenic sources such as agricultural and mining...
steps.searchable_summary: interpret PCA components and loadings to infer sources
inputs: External data (e.g., Enrichment Factors, background concentrations)
outputs: Inferred sources for each PCA component (e.g., anthropogenic, lithogenic, mixed)

steps.description: The minor enrichment for Cu and Zn in the study area may have resulted from low anthropogenic inputs, including domestic/municipal wastewaters and vehicle emissions... In addition, fertilizer and pesticide application in agriculture can be considered as the primary source of Cu and Zn... The concentration of Fe was considered to result from natural sources... In previous studies, it was stated that the main sources of Ni and Cr in sediment are parent materials and pedogenic process... The minor enrichment for Pb in the study area may have resulted from low anthropogenic inputs, including vehicle emissions and mine reserves... It is known that Fe and Mn metals are naturally occurring metals in the earth's crust. These metals come from natural resources such as coastal erosion and rock weathering.
steps.searchable_summary: discuss potential sources of heavy metals based on statistical analysis and literature
inputs: Inferred sources from PCA and correlation
Literature on heavy metal sources in the region
outputs: Discussion of potential sources of heavy metals in sediment
